meta.title: QEEPP Full Diagnostic Assessment
meta.description: Evidence-based full diagnostic assessment aligned to the QEEPP Practical Assessment Framework.

answerScale.label: Not evident
answerScale.description: No credible evidence found in the assessed scope.

answerScale.label: Isolated / informal
answerScale.description: Some isolated signs exist, but the control is informal or unreliable.

answerScale.label: Partial / inconsistent
answerScale.description: The control exists in parts of the scope, but application is uneven or incomplete.

answerScale.label: Defined / repeatable
answerScale.description: The control is defined and applied in a repeatable way across the assessed scope.

answerScale.label: Governed / measured
answerScale.description: The control is monitored, governed, and used in management or delivery decisions.

answerScale.label: Embedded / self-sustaining
answerScale.description: The control is broadly embedded, consistently sustained, and structurally dependable.

controls.label: Architecture baseline
controls.description: Assess whether architecture is defined, repeatable, and governed consistently.
controls.checkpoints: Target architecture exists, standards are visible, review mechanisms are used, and platform sprawl is understood.
controls.assessmentActions: Review architecture documents, inspect standards, examine review decisions, and compare deployed patterns against the intended architecture baseline.
scoringGuidance: 5: architecture discipline is embedded across the assessed scope

questions.text: Is there a defined target architecture for the transformation?
questions.help: Look for a documented target architecture, reference patterns, standards, platform principles, and a visible intended future state.

questions.text: Are reference architectures and standards documented?
questions.help: Check whether standards are explicit, visible to teams, and consistently available where delivery decisions are made.

questions.text: Are architectural decisions governed consistently?
questions.help: Review architecture review boards, exception handling, decision records, and whether architecture materially influences real delivery choices.

controls.label: Security guardrails
controls.description: Assess whether security expectations are integrated into delivery and operationalized through visible controls.
controls.checkpoints: Security expectations are defined, pipeline controls exist, and vulnerabilities are monitored and acted on.
controls.assessmentActions: Inspect security policies, review pipeline controls, examine vulnerability dashboards, and confirm whether security reviews occur during delivery.
scoringGuidance: 2: controls exist but are partial or informal
3: security practices are defined and repeatable
4: security is actively governed and measured
5: security guardrails are embedded and self-sustaining

questions.text: Are security controls integrated into delivery practices?
questions.help: Look for security checks in pipelines, change processes, pull-request reviews, release governance, and operational workflows.

questions.text: Are security guardrails defined and understood?
questions.help: Check for clear security expectations, defined policies, secure defaults, platform guardrails, and shared understanding across teams.

questions.text: Is vulnerability management active and visible?
questions.help: Inspect vulnerability dashboards, remediation workflows, review cadences, and whether issues are acted on rather than merely reported.

controls.label: Data foundations
controls.description: Assess whether data ownership, governance, and quality are treated as a visible structural discipline.
controls.checkpoints: Ownership is assigned, governance is defined, and data quality is treated as an operational discipline rather than an afterthought.
controls.assessmentActions: Review data ownership and stewardship, inspect governance artifacts, and verify whether quality issues are monitored and escalated consistently.
scoringGuidance: 4: data quality and governance are actively managed
5: data foundations are embedded at scale

questions.text: Is data ownership clearly defined?
questions.help: Look for named owners, stewardship roles, accountable teams, and clarity on who governs critical data domains.

questions.text: Are governance responsibilities and quality expectations visible?
questions.help: Check whether data rules, stewardship expectations, issue handling, and quality responsibilities are documented and understood.

questions.text: Are data standards applied consistently?
questions.help: Review schema standards, data controls, quality checks, lineage practices, and whether teams follow them in operational reality.

controls.label: Reliability engineering
controls.description: Assess whether service expectations, resilience, and learning from failure are engineered intentionally.
controls.checkpoints: Reliability targets exist, resilience is designed intentionally, and operational learning is visible after incidents.
controls.assessmentActions: Review SLA or SLO records, inspect resilience patterns, evaluate outage history, and verify the presence of recovery testing and post-incident review.
scoringGuidance: 5: reliability is engineered and institutionally governed

questions.text: Are uptime and service expectations defined?
questions.help: Look for SLAs, SLOs, availability targets, service tiers, and explicit expectations about reliability.

questions.text: Are resilience and recovery mechanisms tested?
questions.help: Check failover design, recovery procedures, backup validation, resilience exercises, and proof that mechanisms are actually tested.

questions.text: Are failures reviewed systematically?
questions.help: Inspect incident reviews, root-cause follow-up, learning loops, and whether systemic improvements happen after outages or degradations.

controls.label: Technical debt and risk
controls.description: Assess whether structural debt and technical risk are visible, reviewed, and credibly prioritized.
controls.checkpoints: Technical debt is not hidden, risk is visible, and structural remediation competes credibly with feature pressure.
controls.assessmentActions: Inspect risk registers, review backlog tagging, examine remediation plans, and confirm whether debt is discussed in governance conversations.
scoringGuidance: 5: embedded discipline with sustained structural control

questions.text: Is technical debt visible and quantified?
questions.help: Look for debt registers, backlog tagging, quantified exposure, architecture concerns, and visibility beyond verbal acknowledgement.

questions.text: Are structural risks captured and reviewed?
questions.help: Check for risk registers, review forums, escalation paths, and whether risks are revisited systematically.

questions.text: Does delivery planning account for debt reduction?
questions.help: Review sprint, release, and portfolio planning to see whether remediation work competes credibly with feature pressure.

controls.label: Strategy alignment
controls.description: Assess whether transformation work is visibly tied to strategy and understood in business terms.
controls.checkpoints: Strategy is visible in the assessment scope and transformation effort is not disconnected from business priorities.
controls.assessmentActions: Review strategic plans, roadmaps, and executive messaging. Test whether initiatives can be traced clearly to strategic objectives.
scoringGuidance: 2: weak or inconsistent linkage to strategy

questions.text: Are initiatives explicitly tied to strategic objectives?
questions.help: Look for clear traceability from initiatives to strategic goals, business priorities, or target outcomes.

questions.text: Is strategic intent visible in the transformation scope?
questions.help: Check roadmaps, charters, funding logic, and executive communications for visible strategic framing.

questions.text: Can leadership explain why this work matters in business terms?
questions.help: Test whether leaders can explain the work through business outcomes, not just delivery milestones or technical activity.

controls.label: Capability mapping
controls.description: Assess whether transformation scope is framed through business capabilities and visible dependencies.
controls.checkpoints: Capability language is used consistently and dependencies are visible enough to guide decisions.
controls.assessmentActions: Review capability maps, dependency views, and transformation roadmaps. Confirm whether capability strengthening is explicit and traceable.
scoringGuidance: 4: capabilities drive planning and dependency decisions
5: capability mapping is embedded in transformation governance

questions.text: Are business capabilities mapped to the transformation scope?
questions.help: Look for capability maps, capability heatmaps, target-state models, and explicit links between work and capability improvement.

questions.text: Are dependencies visible across business and technology?
questions.help: Check whether cross-functional dependencies are mapped, discussed, and used to inform sequencing or decisions.

questions.text: Is the change framed in capability terms, not only project terms?
questions.help: Review planning materials and governance language to see whether capability outcomes matter beyond project delivery status.

controls.label: Value stream alignment
controls.description: Assess whether work is organized around value delivery rather than remaining trapped in silos.
controls.checkpoints: Value streams are explicit enough to influence ownership, decision-making, and prioritization.
controls.assessmentActions: Review value stream maps, product ownership structures, and operating model decisions to see whether value flow shapes delivery.
scoringGuidance: 1: siloed work with little value visibility
2: value streams discussed but weakly applied
4: value streams shape governance and delivery
5: value flow is embedded across the assessed scope

questions.text: Are value streams clearly identified?
questions.help: Look for named value streams, product lines, customer journeys, or service flows that meaningfully structure the work.

questions.text: Do teams and initiatives align to value delivery?
questions.help: Check whether ownership, teams, and decision-making align to value flow instead of fragmented functional boundaries.

questions.text: Is work organized beyond functional silos?
questions.help: Review governance, team design, and operating patterns for evidence that value flow shapes delivery in practice.

controls.label: Outcome definition
controls.description: Assess whether success is defined in measurable outcomes rather than only activity or delivery output.
controls.checkpoints: Outcomes are explicit, measurable, and used to distinguish real business progress from mere activity.
controls.assessmentActions: Inspect KPI sets, OKRs, or outcome statements. Verify whether outcomes are reviewed and influence decisions.
scoringGuidance: 2: partial metrics with weak business meaning

questions.text: Are measurable outcomes defined?
questions.help: Look for explicit outcome statements, target measures, KPIs, OKRs, and links to business value.

questions.text: Are success criteria framed beyond delivery activity?
questions.help: Check whether success is defined by actual impact, not just milestones, launches, or completion counts.

questions.text: Do OKRs or equivalent measures exist?
questions.help: Review the presence, clarity, and practical use of OKRs, outcome metrics, or equivalent decision-support measures.

controls.label: Initiative prioritization
controls.description: Assess whether work is prioritized by value and structural readiness rather than politics or noise.
controls.checkpoints: Prioritization is not purely political or reactive and can be explained through strategic and structural logic.
controls.assessmentActions: Review governance forums, funding discussions, and portfolio decisions to test how trade-offs are actually made.
scoringGuidance: 5: portfolio choice is disciplined and consistently value-led

questions.text: Are initiatives prioritized by value and structural readiness?
questions.help: Look for prioritization frameworks that consider expected value, strategic relevance, dependencies, and readiness constraints.

questions.text: Is prioritization visible and explainable?
questions.help: Check whether trade-offs are documented, governance logic is transparent, and choices can be explained credibly.

questions.text: Can lower-value work be delayed in favor of more important outcomes?
questions.help: Review actual portfolio decisions for evidence that more important work displaces less valuable work when needed.

controls.label: Operating model
controls.description: Assess whether the operating model is clear enough to support efficient execution with manageable friction.
controls.checkpoints: Roles, ownership, and governance pathways are clear enough to support efficient execution.
controls.assessmentActions: Review operating model artifacts, team boundaries, and decision forums. Test whether ownership is explicit and workable in practice.
scoringGuidance: 5: operating model is embedded and scalable

questions.text: Is the operating model clearly defined?
questions.help: Look for documented operating model principles, team boundaries, governance pathways, and role clarity.

questions.text: Are decision rights and responsibilities understood?
questions.help: Check whether ownership is explicit, decision paths are visible, and teams understand who is accountable for what.

questions.text: Do handoffs introduce unnecessary friction?
questions.help: Review real delivery flow for blocked decisions, waiting, rework, duplicated approvals, or unclear interfaces between teams.

controls.label: FinOps discipline
controls.description: Assess whether cost visibility and optimization discipline are active and influence design or operational choices.
controls.checkpoints: Cost transparency exists and operational or platform decisions are influenced by cost insight.
controls.assessmentActions: Inspect cost dashboards, showback or chargeback logic, and optimization review cadence to verify whether cost discipline is real.
scoringGuidance: 2: cost review is reactive or partial
5: cost discipline is embedded in delivery behavior

questions.text: Are costs baselined and visible?
questions.help: Look for cost dashboards, service baselines, tagging, ownership views, and accessible reporting of spend.

questions.text: Is optimization continuous rather than occasional?
questions.help: Check whether review cadence, optimization backlogs, or governance patterns make cost management ongoing.

questions.text: Do teams understand the cost impact of design choices?
questions.help: Review whether engineering, platform, or product teams can see and discuss cost implications of architecture and delivery decisions.

controls.label: Application rationalization
controls.description: Assess whether duplication and platform sprawl are being reduced through governed structural decisions.
controls.checkpoints: Rationalization is more than inventory. There is visible reduction of duplication and improvement of coherence.
controls.assessmentActions: Review application inventories, rationalization plans, and retirement decisions. Confirm whether consolidation activity is real and governed.
scoringGuidance: 2: inventory exists but action is weak
5: rationalization is embedded in structural decision-making

questions.text: Are redundant applications or platforms being reduced?
questions.help: Look for retirement plans, decommissioning activity, consolidation programs, and evidence of actual reduction.

questions.text: Is platform sprawl understood and addressed?
questions.help: Check inventories, architecture views, ownership clarity, and whether sprawl is visible enough to manage.

questions.text: Are rationalization decisions linked to transformation goals?
questions.help: Review whether rationalization supports strategic outcomes, operating simplification, or target-state architecture.

controls.label: DevSecOps flow
controls.description: Assess whether delivery pipelines and controls create repeatable, measurable, and integrated flow.
controls.checkpoints: Delivery flow is visible, repeatable, and increasingly standardized across the assessment scope.
controls.assessmentActions: Review pipeline designs, cycle-time metrics, control gates, and exception handling to assess the integrity of delivery flow.
scoringGuidance: 3: DevSecOps flow is defined and repeatable
5: flow is embedded, standardized, and sustainable

questions.text: Are delivery pipelines standardized?
questions.help: Look for common pipeline patterns, release stages, control gates, reusable workflows, and reduced variation where appropriate.

questions.text: Is flow measured and improved over time?
questions.help: Check cycle-time metrics, lead times, bottlenecks, DORA-style measures, and evidence of flow improvement over time.

questions.text: Are security and control integrated into the delivery process?
questions.help: Review whether security checks, approval gates, and controls are embedded in flow rather than bolted on afterward.

controls.label: Automation
controls.description: Assess whether automation materially reduces manual friction and improves structural consistency.
controls.checkpoints: Automation is systematic enough to improve consistency, speed, and control rather than merely providing isolated technical convenience.
controls.assessmentActions: Inspect infrastructure as code repositories, orchestration patterns, and operational workflows to verify whether automation changes the way work is done.
scoringGuidance: 2: isolated automation with limited structural impact
5: automation is embedded and scaled across the scope

questions.text: Are repetitive tasks automated intentionally?
questions.help: Look for systematic automation of repetitive operational or delivery tasks rather than isolated convenience scripts.

questions.text: Is infrastructure provisioned through governed mechanisms?
questions.help: Check for infrastructure as code, policy guardrails, approved modules, and governed provisioning workflows.

questions.text: Does automation reduce manual friction materially?
questions.help: Review whether automation changes delivery behavior, reduces waiting, errors, or inconsistency, and is visible in practice.

controls.label: KPI framework
controls.description: Assess whether transformation KPIs are meaningful, relevant, and active in governance conversations.
controls.checkpoints: KPIs are explicit, relevant, and active in leadership discussions rather than decorative reporting elements.
controls.assessmentActions: Review scorecards, governance packs, and reporting definitions to confirm whether KPI language is operationalized.
scoringGuidance: 2: partial metrics with weak governance use
5: KPI discipline is embedded across the organization

questions.text: Are transformation KPIs clearly defined?
questions.help: Look for explicit KPI definitions, owners, calculation logic, and stable reporting measures.

questions.text: Do the KPIs reflect structural integrity rather than only activity?
questions.help: Check whether metrics illuminate quality, risk, stability, outcomes, or control integrity, not just delivery volume.

questions.text: Are the KPIs used in governance conversations?
questions.help: Review scorecards, governance packs, and meeting outputs for evidence that KPIs influence oversight and decisions.

controls.label: Service metrics
controls.description: Assess whether service expectations and service performance data are visible and actively used.
controls.checkpoints: Service expectations are explicit and used to guide operations, reliability, and governance decisions.
controls.assessmentActions: Inspect service definitions, reliability metrics, and operational reviews to verify whether service-level management is functioning consistently.
scoringGuidance: 4: service performance is monitored and governed
5: service management is embedded and mature

questions.text: Are SLA or SLO expectations defined?
questions.help: Look for service targets, reliability thresholds, support commitments, and explicit expectations for service behavior.

questions.text: Are reliability and service measures visible?
questions.help: Check dashboards, reports, and operational views for uptime, latency, error rates, incident metrics, or related service indicators.

questions.text: Do teams respond consistently to service performance data?
questions.help: Review whether service data triggers action, remediation, prioritization, or governance follow-up.

controls.label: Execution cadence
controls.description: Assess whether governance and review rhythm is reliable enough to create accountability and action.
controls.checkpoints: Governance rhythm exists and contributes to accountability rather than producing only ceremonial meetings.
controls.assessmentActions: Review governance calendars, meeting outputs, and decision logs to test whether cadence is active and consequential.
scoringGuidance: 5: cadence is embedded and structurally reliable

questions.text: Are governance reviews held regularly?
questions.help: Look for scheduled forums, established review cycles, and predictable governance touchpoints.

questions.text: Is there a reliable rhythm for assessing progress?
questions.help: Check whether review cadence is dependable, timely, and actually sustained over time.

questions.text: Do reviews lead to decisions and action?
questions.help: Review decisions, action logs, escalations, and follow-through to verify that cadence is consequential rather than ceremonial.

controls.label: Risk and compliance reporting
controls.description: Assess whether risks and compliance exceptions are visible early enough to support timely corrective action.
controls.checkpoints: Risks and compliance exposures are surfaced in time to support corrective action rather than retrospective explanation.
controls.assessmentActions: Review risk registers, control reporting, and escalation practices to confirm that risk visibility is active and not purely formal.
scoringGuidance: 5: risk and compliance visibility is embedded institutionally

questions.text: Are structural risks visible early?
questions.help: Look for leading indicators, early escalation patterns, and reporting that surfaces issues before they become crises.

questions.text: Is compliance reporting reliable and usable?
questions.help: Check whether compliance signals are current, understandable, decision-relevant, and not merely formal artifacts.

questions.text: Can governance bodies see exceptions before they become crises?
questions.help: Review whether exceptions, deviations, or material risks reach decision-makers with enough time to act.

controls.label: Operational transparency
controls.description: Assess whether performance signals are visible, trusted, and sufficient for informed governance.
controls.checkpoints: Operational signals are visible enough to support shared understanding and prompt action across leadership and delivery stakeholders.
controls.assessmentActions: Inspect dashboards, audience access, and reporting practices to test whether transparency is real, timely, and actionable.
scoringGuidance: 4: transparency is monitored and used in governance
5: visibility is embedded and broadly trusted

questions.text: Are dashboards visible and trusted?
questions.help: Look for accessible dashboards, consistent definitions, data trust, and audience confidence in reported signals.

questions.text: Can relevant stakeholders see performance clearly?
questions.help: Check whether leadership, delivery, and operational stakeholders all have access to relevant views when needed.

questions.text: Is transparency sufficient for informed governance decisions?
questions.help: Review whether transparency is timely, actionable, and adequate for governance trade-offs and interventions.

controls.label: Platform enablement
controls.description: Assess whether internal platforms create structural leverage and accelerate delivery across teams.
controls.checkpoints: Platforms are enabling delivery across multiple teams and creating structural leverage rather than isolated technical benefit.
controls.assessmentActions: Review platform services, adoption patterns, and team usage. Test whether platform enablement materially changes delivery capability.
scoringGuidance: 2: partial platform capability with limited adoption
3: platform enablement is defined and usable
4: platforms are managed and broadly adopted
5: platform enablement is embedded and structurally scalable

questions.text: Do internal platforms accelerate delivery in a controlled way?
questions.help: Look for platform services that improve speed, consistency, or safety while remaining governed.

questions.text: Are teams benefiting from common services and patterns?
questions.help: Check adoption of shared services, standard patterns, paved roads, and whether teams actually use them.

questions.text: Is platform enablement reducing duplication?
questions.help: Review whether platforms reduce repeated reinvention, bespoke tooling, or fragmented team-level solutions.

controls.label: Self-service infrastructure
controls.description: Assess whether teams can provision approved capabilities independently within visible guardrails.
controls.checkpoints: Self-service exists within guardrails and expands capability without reducing governance strength.
controls.assessmentActions: Review provisioning models, guardrails, approval patterns, and usage pathways to confirm whether self-service is both real and governed.
scoringGuidance: 5: self-service is embedded, trusted, and scalable

questions.text: Can teams provision approved capabilities independently?
questions.help: Look for self-service pathways, approved templates, internal portals, and reduced dependence on central bottlenecks.

questions.text: Is self-service governed by standards and controls?
questions.help: Check policy guardrails, approval boundaries, secure defaults, and whether self-service remains controlled.

questions.text: Does self-service reduce dependence on central bottlenecks?
questions.help: Review actual workflow evidence to confirm whether self-service improves flow rather than existing only on paper.

controls.label: Reusable components
controls.description: Assess whether reuse is systematic, intentional, and materially reduces delivery duplication.
controls.checkpoints: Reuse is visible in architecture and delivery patterns and contributes materially to scale efficiency.
controls.assessmentActions: Inspect component catalogs, template libraries, and reference implementations to verify whether reuse is systematic and adopted.
scoringGuidance: 2: ad hoc reuse with weak structure
3: reusable assets are defined and repeatable
4: reuse is governed and broadly applied
5: reuse is embedded as a structural operating norm

questions.text: Are patterns, templates, and services reused consistently?
questions.help: Look for catalogs, templates, shared modules, reference implementations, and repeated usage across teams.

questions.text: Does reuse reduce delivery time and duplication?
questions.help: Check whether reuse changes delivery speed, reduces repeated work, and lowers unnecessary variation.

questions.text: Is reuse intentional rather than accidental?
questions.help: Review governance, platform design, or enablement practices to see whether reuse is designed and encouraged deliberately.

controls.label: Delivery enablement
controls.description: Assess whether teams have the standards, tooling, and support needed to deliver consistently without excessive reinvention.
controls.checkpoints: Teams are enabled by shared tooling, guidance, and operational support rather than left to build everything independently.
controls.assessmentActions: Review onboarding paths, enablement assets, standards, and support models to assess whether delivery capability is being scaled systematically.
scoringGuidance: 1: weak delivery support and high reinvention
4: enablement is managed and broadly useful
5: enablement is embedded and structurally scalable

questions.text: Do teams have the standards, tooling, and support needed to deliver well?
questions.help: Look for standards, tooling, onboarding aids, enablement guides, office hours, support channels, and delivery support assets.

questions.text: Is enablement consistent across the assessed scope?
questions.help: Check whether support and guidance are broadly available, not dependent on local heroes or isolated teams.

questions.text: Can teams onboard and operate without excessive reinvention?
questions.help: Review whether new or existing teams can become effective without building everything from scratch.

controls.label: Scalable capacity
controls.description: Assess whether growth in scope or throughput can occur without multiplying complexity and instability.
controls.checkpoints: Growth in scope or throughput does not automatically produce fragmentation, instability, or governance breakdown.
controls.assessmentActions: Review scaling patterns, team growth, operational stability, and governance quality under expansion to determine whether scale is sustainable.
scoringGuidance: 3: scalable capacity is defined and plausible
4: scale is managed and supported by structure
5: scale is sustainable and institutionally supported

questions.text: Can delivery capacity expand without multiplying complexity?
questions.help: Look for scaling mechanisms, modular structures, platform leverage, or operating patterns that support growth cleanly.

questions.text: Does growth preserve control, alignment, and reliability?
questions.help: Check whether expansion maintains governance quality, architectural coherence, and operational stability.

questions.text: Are scale mechanisms visible in practice rather than assumed?
questions.help: Review actual examples of scaling, team growth, throughput expansion, or platform adoption to test whether scale is real and sustainable.
